text:
🎧 YouGlish
To invite someone out casually, the default opening question is: {{c1::"Are you free to grab dinner sometime next week?"}}
explanation:
Native speed: 'ər·jə friː tə græb dɪ·nər sʌm·taɪm nɛks wiːk?'
Rules applied:
• are you → /ər·jə/ (schwa reduction on 'you')
• free to → /friː·tə/ (schwa reduction on 'to')
• grab → /græb/ (colloquial usage for 'get/eat')
• next week → /nɛks wiːk/ (consonant elision: final /t/ of 'next' is elided before the consonant /w/)

🧠 'Are you free...' is low-pressure. Combining it with 'grab' and 'sometime' makes the invitation sound casual and easy to accept.
usage:
• "Are you free to grab a coffee tomorrow afternoon?"
  • "Are you free to hang out this weekend?"
spanish: '¿Estás libre para ir a cenar alguna vez la próxima semana?' — Invitación informal. Fonética: 'are you' se reduce a 'er-juh', 'to' a 'tuh', 'grab' es el verbo coloquial y en 'next week' se omite la /t/ final de 'next' (/nɛks/).

text:
🎧 YouGlish
To cancel or postpone plans politely because of an unexpected event, say: {{c1::"Hey, sorry but something came up. Can we reschedule?"}}
explanation:
Native speed: 'heɪ, sɑː·ri bət sʌm·θɪŋ keɪ·mʌp. kə·nwi ri·skɛ·dʒuːl?'
• something came up → /sʌm·θɪŋ keɪ·mʌp/ (linking C+V: final /m/ of 'came' links to 'up')
• can we → /kə·nwi/ (schwa reduction on 'can')

🧠 'Something came up' is a vague but polite idiomatic phrase used to signal that an unforeseen situation requires your attention, without oversharing personal details.
usage:
• "I'm really sorry to do this, but something came up at work. Can we reschedule for Thursday?"
  • "Something came up last minute, so I won't be able to make it tonight."
spanish: 'Oye, lo siento pero surgió algo. ¿Podemos reprogramar?' — Cancelar o posponer planes cortésmente. 'Something came up' es un modismo para indicar un imprevisto sin dar detalles incómodos. Fonéticamente, 'came up' se enlaza y 'can' se reduce a schwa.
